sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=1, col=1).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 180, level 3.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 125 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 4.8x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 62 actions returned the board to a state already seen 62 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 62 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, I can see the current state:
- Left H: gBBBggBBBggBBBggBBBg (all OFF - 'B' centers)
- Left V: ggwggggwggggwggggwgg (all ON - 'w' centers)
- Right H: wwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwww (mixed - some ON some OFF)
- Right V: WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW (mixed - some ON some OFF)
Preview box 1 shows:
- White dots at rows 56, 58 (top area)
- Yellow at rows 60-61 (bottom area)
Now let me think about this more carefully. The preview boxes toggle left button states when clicked. The left buttons control the LEFT panel. The right buttons control the RIGHT panel.
Looking at the right panel:
- Checkerboard pattern (black/charcoal 4x4 blocks)
- Magenta vertical bar (columns 3-4)
- Yellow square (row 3, column 1)
- Yellow L-shape (rows 1-2, columns 5-6)
What if the goal is to make the LEFT panel match the RIGHT panel? The left panel currently shows a charcoal T-shape on a dark gray background. The right panel shows colored objects on a checkerboard background.
In Level 1, the trigger moved the charcoal shape. In Level 2, the trigger completed the level when right V was all OFF. In Level 3, maybe the trigger needs specific button configs to work.
Let me think about what configuration would make sense:
- The right panel has objects in specific rows and columns
- The buttons control visibility of rows/columns
- The goal might be to turn ON only the rows/columns that contain colored objects
Looking at the right panel more carefully:
- Magenta bar: spans all 6 rows, columns 3-4
- Yellow square: row 3, column 1
- Yellow L-shape: rows 1-2, columns 5-6
So the colored objects appear in:
- Rows: 1, 2, 3 (and magenta spans all rows 1-6)
- Columns: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6
If the goal is to turn ON only the rows/columns with colored objects:
- Right H: all 6 ON (magenta spans all rows)
- Right V: 1, 3, 4, 5, 6 ON (columns with colored objects), 2 OFF
But wait, the magenta bar is in columns 3-4, so columns 3 and 4 have magenta. Column 1 has yellow square. Columns 5-6 have yellow L-shape. Column 2 has only checkerboard.
So: Right V should have buttons 1, 3, 4, 5, 6 ON, button 2 OFF.
And for rows: magenta spans all rows, yellow objects are in rows 1-3. If we turn ON all rows, we see the full magenta bar. If we turn OFF rows 4-6, we only see the top part of the magenta bar.
Hmm, but the goal might be to turn OFF the rows/columns that DON'T have colored objects:
- Right H: turn OFF rows 4-6 (no yellow objects there, but magenta is there)
- Right V: turn OFF column 2 (no colored objects there)
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me try a simple hypothesis:
The goal is to turn OFF all right buttons (both H and V). This would hide all colored objects and show only the checkerboard pattern. Then press the trigger.
Let me try this: set all right buttons OFF, then press trigger.
Current right H: wwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwww (mixed)
Current right V: WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW (mixed)
To turn all OFF, I need to click the buttons that are currently ON.
Right H: 'wwwWW' pattern means buttons 1-3 are ON ('w'), buttons 4-5 are OFF ('W'). Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-read.
Right H: wwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwww
This is 30 characters for 6 buttons, each 5 chars wide:
- Button 1: wwwWW (ON)
- Button 2: wwwWW (ON)
- Button 3: wwwWW (ON)
- Button 4: wwwWW (ON)
- Button 5: wwwWW (ON)
- Button 6: wwwWW (ON)
Wait, that's 6 * 5 = 30 characters, and the pattern is 'wwwWW' repeated 6 times. So all right H buttons are ON.
Right V: WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW
This is 30 characters for 6 buttons, each 5 chars wide:
- Button 1: WwWWW (OFF)
- Button 2: WWwWW (OFF)
- Button 3: WWwWW (OFF)
- Button 4: WWwWW (OFF)
- Button 5: WWwWW (OFF)
- Button 6: WWwWW (OFF)
Wait, let me recount. 'WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW' has 30 characters:
W w W W W | W w W W W | W w W W W | W w W W W | W w W W W | W w W W W
So each button has 'WwWWW' pattern, meaning the center is 'w' (ON). So all right V buttons are ON.
Wait, but earlier I saw right V as 'WBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBWWWWBW' which had 'B' centers. Let me re-check.
Actually, the current state shows: WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW
Let me count: W-w-W-W-W-W-w-W-W-W-W-w-W-W-W-W-w-W-W-W-W-w-W-W-W-W = 29 characters? No, let me be more careful.
'WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW'
W(1) w(2) W(3) W(4) W(5) W(6) w(7) W(8) W(9) W(10) W(11) w(12) W(13) W(14) W(15) W(16) w(17) W(18) W(19) W(20) W(21) w(22) W(23) W(24) W(25) W(26) w(27) W(28) W(29) W(30)
So positions 2, 7, 12, 17, 22, 27 are 'w' (ON). These are the centers of 5-character groups starting at positions 1, 6, 11, 16, 21, 26.
Wait, that's groups of 5: 1-5, 6-10, 11-15, 16-20, 21-25, 26-30.
Centers at: 3, 8, 13, 18, 23, 28.
But the 'w' positions are 2, 7, 12, 17, 22, 27. These are NOT the centers.
Hmm, let me re-examine. The button layout might be different. Let me look at the ascii more carefully.
Actually, I think the button centers are at positions 2, 7, 12, 17, 22, 27 (0-indexed: 1, 6, 11, 16, 21, 26). So each button is 5 characters wide, with the center at position 1 (0-indexed) within the group.
So for Right V: WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW
Groups: [WwWWW] [WwWWW] [WwWWW] [WwWWW] [WwWWW] [WwWWW]
Each group has 'w' at position 1, which is ON. So all right V buttons are ON.
OK so currently all right H and right V buttons are ON. To turn them all OFF, I need to click each one.
But wait - I need to be careful. If I turn all OFF and press trigger, and it causes game over, I've wasted actions.
Let me think about what the correct configuration might be.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole problem. In Level 2, the goal was to turn OFF all right V buttons. The trigger worked when right V was all OFF.
In Level 3, maybe the goal is different. What if the goal is to turn ON specific buttons to reveal a pattern, then press the trigger?
Looking at the preview boxes, they show patterns that might indicate which buttons to turn ON:
- Box 1: yellow at bottom → turn ON bottom buttons?
- Box 2: yellow at top → turn ON top buttons?
- Box 3: yellow on left → turn ON left buttons?
- Box 4: yellow on right → turn ON right buttons?
But the preview boxes toggle LEFT button states, not right button states. So maybe the goal is to set LEFT buttons to match the preview box patterns?
Actually, I just realized something. The preview boxes are at the bottom, and they toggle left button states. But what if they're not just toggling - what if they're showing the TARGET configuration?
Let me look at the preview boxes again:
- Box 1: yellow at bottom (rows 6-7), white dots at top (rows 1-2)
- Box 2: yellow at top (rows 1-2), white dots at bottom (rows 6-7)
- Box 3: yellow on left (cols 1-2), white dots on right (cols 6-7)
- Box 4: yellow on right (cols 6-7), white dots on left (cols 1-2)
These could represent:
- Box 1: V=ON (yellow bottom), H=OFF (white top)
- Box 2: V=OFF (white bottom), H=ON (yellow top)
- Box 3: H=ON (yellow left), V=OFF (white right)
- Box 4: H=OFF (white left), V=ON (yellow right)
But there are 4 boxes and only 2 button types. Maybe each box represents a different combination for the LEFT and RIGHT buttons?
OK I'm going in circles. Let me just try a simple test: set all right buttons OFF, then press trigger. If it works, great. If game over, I'll learn something.
Actually wait - I should NOT press the trigger until I'm confident about the configuration, because each wrong press causes game over and wastes actions.
Let me try a different approach: what if the goal is NOT to press the trigger at all? What if the goal is to set the buttons to a specific configuration, and the level completes automatically?
Let me try: set all right buttons OFF, and see if the level completes without pressing the trigger.
To turn all right H OFF: click all 6 right H buttons (they're currently all ON)
To turn all right V OFF: click all 6 right V buttons (they're currently all ON)
That's 12 actions. If the level completes, great. If not, I'll try pressing the trigger.
Actually, let me be even more careful. Let me first turn OFF just the right V buttons (6 actions), and see if the level completes. In Level 2, turning OFF all right V buttons completed the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: all right H and right V buttons are ON
# Let me try turning OFF all right V buttons first (like Level 2)
# Right V button centers are at row 47, cols 34, 39, 44, 49, 54, 59

# Turn off all 6 right V buttons
actions = []
for col in [34, 39, 44, 49, 54, 59]:
    actions.append({'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 47, 'col': col})

action(actions)
print(f"After turning off right V: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed', False)}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Check if level completed
if last_action_result.get('level_completed', False):
    print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
else:
    # Check button states
    lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    print(f"Right V now: {lines[47][33:61]}")
    print(f"Right H now: {lines[33][33:61]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Right H now: wwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwwwWWwww
